<findings>

[suggestion] [warning] Zone "PRD Validation" (packages-rex:prd-validation) has critical risk (score: 0.64, cohesion: 0.36, coupling: 0.64) — requires refactoring before new feature development

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.36) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.64) — 20 imports target "analyze-engine"
- Two distinct validation modes (batch and interactive) are separated into distinct command files — a clean UX boundary that allows the interactive workflow to evolve independently.
- Cohesion (0.36) and coupling (0.64) are both near their respective warning thresholds, driven by the zone drawing on both structural graph utilities and the Analyze Engine for its checks.
- The epic-correlation utility is a specialized semantic check distinct from structural validation; if validation rules grow, extracting a src/core/validators/ directory would prevent this zone from becoming a dumping ground for all rule logic.
- Coupling of 0.64 slightly exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; the 20 imports from unit-analyze suggest validation rules are tightly coupled to the internal graph model — a stable ValidationContext interface would reduce this dependency.
- The interactive validate command (validate-interactive.ts) coexists with the batch command in the same zone — this separation of UX modes is intentional and well-structured, mirroring the same pattern used in other CLI commands.
- epic-correlation.ts represents a semantic validation concern (cross-item relationship checking) distinct from structural.ts (graph integrity) — keeping these as separate files rather than one monolithic validator is good for testability and future extensibility.
- Zone "prd-validation" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- With 20 outgoing imports into unit-analyze and only 1 return import, the validation zone is the cleanest large-consumer boundary in the codebase — its near-unidirectional relationship with the analyze engine is the model other high-coupling zones should target
- validate → unit-analyze (20 imports) with only 1 return import is the healthiest large-volume dependency relationship in the codebase. This zone demonstrates that high coupling counts are tolerable when the flow is strictly unidirectional.
- structural.ts imports only src/schema/index.js and sibling core modules (tree.ts, collectAllIds) — no cross-layer dependencies at the file-content level. The validation zone's coupling score (0.64) is a count artifact reflecting the number of schema constants used, not a structural layering violation; all imports are correctly unidirectional.
- The validation zone's high coupling score (0.64, 20 imports from analyze-engine) is misleading: file-content inspection confirms all imports are schema constants and pure core utilities with no layering violations. Consider annotating this zone as a 'healthy high-volume consumer' to prevent it from being treated as a refactoring target alongside the genuinely problematic circular dependency zones.
- [call graph] 466 internal calls, 75 outgoing, 3 incoming (cohesion: 0.86, coupling: 0.14)

</insights>
